# What the Architect Owns {#sec-what-architect-owns}

```{=latex}
\abstract*{Architecture 2.0 changes the architect's work, but it does not make decision rights self-assigning. This chapter returns to the opening moonshot and asks what may be delegated, what must remain assigned, and how a reader demonstrates transfer on a new problem. Models, tools, specialists, and automated checks may participate throughout a study. Named people or organizational roles still define scope, authority, evidence sufficiency, escalation, waivers, and the commit right for consequential action.}
```

::: {.callout-crux}
When AI systems, tools, and specialists can participate across a bounded
architecture study, which work may be delegated, which decision rights must
remain visibly assigned, and how can a reader show that the practice transfers?
:::

The lighthouse prompt introduced in @sec-moonshot can now be read as a study
brief rather than a request for a future hardware foundation
model[^fn-arch-hardware-foundation-model]. A credible response must connect the
request to alternatives, artifacts, tools, experiments, explanations, and a
decision with a named owner.

[^fn-arch-hardware-foundation-model]: The phrase names a tempting but incomplete reading of the lighthouse prompt, a single broadly trained hardware model that maps intent to designs. Such a model would still need an architecture study grounded in representations, tools, experiments, evidence, challenge, and human judgment. When that work iterates, a design loop organizes its execution and analysis.

Such a study includes workload definition, architecture representations, tool
interfaces, method roles, feedback budgets, evidence records, independent checks,
and authorized decisions. A design loop connects these elements when the work
requires repeated proposal, evaluation, rejection, and revision. Cards,
replay packages, and checks help inspect the work, but the architectural finding or
decision remains the point. AI systems do not eliminate the computer architect.
They extend how architects formulate problems, construct alternatives, direct
tool-backed work, interpret evidence, and make commit decisions.

## The Architect Owns the Decision

AI assistance broadens the architect's responsibility across the study, but not
every check has to move through one person's hands. Simulators, policy checks,
and regression suites may reject candidates or advance routine transitions
automatically. The architect or authorized organizational role defines the
scope of that automation, what each check may block or advance, the evidence needed
for each class of decision, the escalation and waiver paths, and the point at
which exercising the commit right requires recorded acceptance. The work can be
distributed, but responsibility for the decision must remain assigned.

Here, *the architect* means the role responsible for the decision, not
necessarily one individual performing every operation. Large programs may
assign workload scope, software compatibility, physical signoff, security, and
product acceptance to different authorized roles. The study remains governed
only when those rights, handoffs, and limits are assigned. No heroic generalist
is required. Each responsibility must remain explicit as work crosses models,
tools, teams, and abstraction levels.

That responsibility is especially sharp near tapeout authorization. A generative
model or an automated optimizer is not answerable for a tapeout decision. Some software
systems are also hard to roll back, but tapeout makes the decision unusually
difficult to reverse. A bug committed to silicon can require a respin and substantial
schedule delay against a design investment that industry estimates put at
roughly \$725M at 2\ nm [@Shilov2023IBSDesignCost].
The 2024 Wilson Research Group study reports first-silicon success at 14
percent among its respondents [@Foster2025WilsonVerification2024]. That is a
high-consequence outcome measure, not a project-specific respin probability or
a diagnosis of why a first spin failed. A named tapeout or product role
therefore has to own the acceptance criteria, unresolved waivers, and commit
decision in the record.
The architect may no longer craft every artifact by hand. The role still helps
formulate the architecture question, develop consequential alternatives,
interpret cross-layer effects, and review the evidence path that supports the
commit decision while remaining answerable for the artifact's consequences.

::: {.callout-learning-objectives}
After this chapter you can assign decision rights across an
AI-assisted architecture study. That means you can:

- **Distinguish** routine checks and transitions that may be automated from decision rights that must remain assigned and answerable;
- **Map** evidence to the consequence it can support without treating fidelity as authority;
- **Explain** why an architect must understand delegated abstractions, tools, and evidence without personally executing every step;
- **Answer** the strongest objections to Architecture 2.0 without treating automation as ownership;
- **Complete** a study path another architect can review, from scoped question to a decision with a named owner;
- **Test** transfer on a new problem against the six capabilities introduced in the Preface.
:::

## Return to the Moonshot

The lighthouse prompt makes these shifted responsibilities concrete:

> Design a low-power, 64-bit RISC-V-based compute subsystem for an
> XRBench-class real-time mobile XR workload. Realize it as a vector-capable
> CPU, accelerator, or SoC block under a 3\ W TDP target in a 3\ nm-class LP
> mobile process, and return a design-space report with evidence and rejected
> alternatives.

The request depends on workload, software, architecture, and physical
constraints that are not fully stated in the prompt. An AI-assisted study must
make those assumptions explicit and define the checks that can reject a
candidate.

::: {.callout-lighthouse title="The prompt is now a study brief"}
"XRBench-class" requires a versioned workload and a stated coverage claim.
"64-bit RISC-V-based" requires compatible compiler, simulator, and instruction
semantics. The CPU, accelerator, and SoC alternatives imply different
integration choices. The 3\ W and process targets require power, thermal, and
physical evidence appropriate to the proposed action. The report should retain
comparisons and rejected alternatives. The decision owner determines what is
fixed, what automation may change, which checks can stop the work, and who may
authorize the next step.
:::

Recording evidence does not create an authority hierarchy. Proxies,
cycle-level simulation, RTL verification, emulation, synthesis,
place-and-route, and signoff checks differ in scope, cost, assumptions, and
independence. Their adequacy depends on the claim and proposed transition.
@tbl-evidence-by-consequence maps evidence to consequence, while
@tbl-decision-rights-matrix separates propose, execute, reject, waive,
recommend, and commit rights. Stronger evidence does not silently assign any of
those rights.

The mapping must respect one more difference between the levels. Public sources
illuminate several consequences of high-consequence decisions, but they do not price the same
event. A proxy sweep reports compute time, a verification survey reports a share
of project schedule, node estimates describe whole-design investment, and an
Intel filing records one company's charge for a field defect. Plotting those
quantities on one dollar axis would imply a common denominator the sources do
not provide (@tbl-consequence-evidence).

| **Decision context** | **What the cited observation measures** | **What it does not measure** | **Decision implication** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Exploratory search | ArchGym reports that a proxy-backed sweep of roughly 4,000 experiments could be rerun in about two days [@KrishnanEtAl2023ArchGym]. | A dollar price for rerunning any architecture study | Search can be repeated cheaply only within the declared tool and workload boundary. |
| Experimental rework | The Wilson study reports verification taking a 50 to 60 percent median share of project schedule [@Foster2022WilsonVerificationStudy]. | The marginal time or cost of reversing one experimental decision | A study should state which verification resource a new candidate consumes. |
| Implementation and integration | Public estimates range from roughly \$175M for a 10 nm design to more than \$540M at 5 nm; a secondary report gives an IBS estimate of \$725M for a large 2 nm chip developed from scratch without reusable IP [@BauerEtAl2020SemiconductorDesignManufacturing; @Shilov2023IBSDesignCost]. | A mask-set price, respin price, or amount lost when one decision changes | Late decisions can put accumulated program investment at risk, but the amount must be estimated for the actual project. |
| Field defect | Intel reported a \$475M pretax charge covering replacement and other costs associated with the Pentium floating-point divide problem [@Intel1995PentiumCharge]. | A universal recall or fleet-rollback cost | An irreversible action needs an owner and a reversal plan proportionate to its actual consequence. |

: **Consequence evidence has different denominators.** The cited observations
bound different questions and cannot be read as one quantitative cost ladder.
Their shared lesson is to name the exposed asset, reversal path, and evidence
required for the proposed action. {#tbl-consequence-evidence tbl-colwidths="[17,27,25,21]"}

The observations do not establish that every more consequential transition has a
predictable dollar multiplier. They establish why commit and waive rights need
especially clear assignment when actions become harder to reverse. The
mapping must name the actual consequence, not borrow a number from
a different project or event.

The requested deliverable also matters. A design-space report is different
from an RTL artifact. RTL functional verification is different from a
placed-and-routed implementation that has passed timing and physical signoff.
Logical correctness alone does not establish routability or satisfaction of
timing, power-integrity, and thermal constraints. Each step changes the evidence
standard. The same prompt can support a brainstorming study, a
research prototype, a simulator-backed design-space exploration, or a
high-consequence implementation study. Treating all of those deliverables as
the same is one of the fastest ways to overclaim.

The design-loop card in @sec-architecture-20-ontology and
@sec-appendix-b-design-loop-card converts the prompt into reviewable questions.
What task and comparison are being studied? Which state and actions are
represented? Which tools, methods, and feedback are available? What evidence
supports the claim, what would contradict it, and which alternatives failed?
Who may stop, waive, reopen, or approve the work?

The answers define which parts of the design space an automated method may
inspect or change. A representation that is too narrow can exclude useful
alternatives. One that grants broad actions without constraints and checks can
let the method optimize the wrong objective or cross a decision boundary.

::: {.callout-architect-checkpoint title="Check the Allowed Actions"}
Has a named role provided constraints, evidence checks, and stop conditions for
every setting the automated optimizer can change? If not, it may optimize an
invalid metric or cross an authority boundary unnoticed. If L2 cache size is a tunable action, an area-budget check
can reject oversize configurations, while a congestion check can screen
candidates likely to be unroutable.
:::

Enforcing those boundaries marks the shift from prompt to a defined study. The
prompt motivates the work. The study records the architecture question,
alternatives, tool path, evidence, explanation, and decision.
Prompt-to-chip asks whether a system can produce an impressive final artifact
from a sentence. A better test asks whether the study preserves
enough of the claim-producing work for another architect to understand what the
artifact establishes. When the work iterates, a design loop is one process
for preserving that path.

## Decision Rights Must Remain Assigned

Even the most detailed study leaves decision rights that cannot be orphaned.
Models can propose, harnesses can execute, tools or reviewers can reject, policy
engines can route routine transitions, specialists can recommend, and an
authorized role can commit. @tbl-decision-rights-matrix keeps those assignments
independent. Holding one right does not imply the others, and no participant
assigns itself authority by being technically capable.

The rights may be distributed across authorized organizational roles. What
cannot disappear is answerability for framing the question, accepting a waiver,
or authorizing a consequential transition. A model can help prepare those
decisions; it cannot answer for the organizational outcome.

::: {.callout-architect-checkpoint title="Trace Delegated Authority"}
When splitting an architecture task across multiple automated participants,
trace each decision right. Which system or tool may halt or advance a routine
transition? Which observation requires escalation, who may grant a waiver, and
which role holds the commit right? Adding more
participants must not make those assignments disappear.
:::

Failures in other high-stakes fields show the cost of independent challenge
arriving after consequential use.

::: {.callout-field-note title="Challenge After Consequential Use"}
In clinical genomics, published predictors were already being used to allocate
patients in clinical trials. Baggerly and Coombes reconstructed the analyses and
found reversed labels, duplicated and inconsistently labeled samples, and other
simple errors, some of which persisted through published corrections
[@BaggerlyCoombes2009].

Independent challenge needs a defined route to the decision owner before or
during consequential use.
:::

That review path requires people who understand the relevant tools. An
architect need not write every harness, inspect every timing path, or
run every place-and-route job. The team must retain named experts
who understand the relevant abstractions, tool contracts, evidence limits, and
likely failure modes well enough to set acceptance criteria, challenge
anomalous results, judge a waiver, and know when a stronger check is required.
A program that cannot produce someone who can explain what an automated check
establishes, what it misses, and why it may advance a claim cannot responsibly
delegate authority to that check. That requirement runs into a classic irony of
automation. Delegation removes the routine work that once trained the experts
the delegation still requires [@Bainbridge1983Ironies]. Training and assessment
are therefore part of the implementation problem, as discussed in
@sec-what-a-graduate-can-do.

This responsibility is cross-layer. A future architect may
not be only a CPU architect, accelerator architect, compiler specialist, or
physical-design expert. Specialists and automated systems can operate pieces of
the stack; authorized roles compose their decisions by determining which
hardware settings may change, which software contracts remain fixed, which
workload scenarios matter, which checks may block a result, and which deployment
consequences are acceptable.

Leveson's central move in safety engineering was to treat safety as a property
of interactions in a sociotechnical control structure rather than the sum of
reliable components [@Leveson2012EngineeringSaferWorld]. The transfer here is
limited because most architecture studies are not safety cases. Still, a
capable model or a passing tool check cannot establish an architectural claim
on its own. Another architect can evaluate the claim only when the study connects
scope, representations, tools, evidence, challenge, and assigned decision
rights.

This distinction separates delegated work from responsibilities that must stay assigned
(@fig-architect-owned-boundary). Automated methods may represent state,
generate candidates, evaluate proxies, call tools, critique results, summarize
evidence, preserve provenance, and reject candidates under declared rules.
Those are substantial contributions. They may propose scope, evidence policies,
or escalation paths, but they cannot legitimately assign themselves
organizational authority, grant themselves waivers, or become answerable for
product risk. An automated optimizer might tune core parameters against a
thermal objective, for instance, while a package or
product role remains responsible for the rule that blocks a reliability or
certification violation and for any waiver that permits the design to advance.

![**Delegated work and assigned responsibility.** Within a defined study, AI systems, tools, and specialists may assist formulation, exploration, implementation, evaluation, explanation, and defense. Named roles remain responsible for scope, abstraction, evidence standards, automation authority, escalation, final approval, and reopening.](images/F11-architect-owned-boundary){#fig-architect-owned-boundary width="100%" fig-alt="Architecture study containing the six architecture capabilities, delegated assistance, an architectural claim for review, supporting instruments, and a full-width band of architect-owned governance obligations."}

The decision rights in @tbl-decision-rights-matrix apply across the study, not
to a single capability. @tbl-architect-responsibilities lists the corresponding
responsibilities and records. Semiconductor programs already use signoff
checklists for artifacts. The table addresses the earlier work that produces
the claims those artifacts are intended to support, including scope, evidence,
automation, waivers, and final approval.

| **Governance obligation** | **Why assignment matters** | **What may be automated or delegated** | **Required record** |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| **Intent, scope, and constraints** | The study must serve a real objective rather than an available benchmark or proxy. | Elicit constraints, compare formulations, run policy checks, and reject declared violations. | Problem statement, non-goals, workload slice, hard constraints, and responsible role. |
| **Abstraction and representation** | Encoded state determines what participants can see, optimize, ignore, or simplify incorrectly. | Translate artifacts, organize traces, find gaps, and propose structured schemas. | Architecture schema, exposed design space, assumptions, hidden-state list, and redaction boundary. |
| **Evidence sufficiency** | Evidence must match the consequence, reversibility, and cost of being wrong. | Gather measurements, track provenance, estimate uncertainty, replay baselines, and route anomalies. | Tool and environment contracts, calibration records, evidence summary, validity scope, and escalation threshold. |
| **Authority, escalation, and waivers** | AI-assisted work needs declared rules for who or what may stop, advance, reopen, or override a decision. | Apply validated checks, reject invalid candidates, advance routine transitions, and notify reviewers. | Authority matrix, automated-check configuration, exception record, waiver rationale, expiration, and reviewer. |
| **Commit-right assignment and answerability** | Product, tapeout, deployment, safety, security, cost, and lifecycle consequences need an answerable owner. | Summarize tradeoffs, check completeness, monitor drift, and trigger rollback or revalidation. | Decision note, accepted and rejected alternatives, residual risks, authorized transition, ownership transfer, and reopening condition. |

: **Responsibilities and required records.** Execution may be automated or delegated, while scope, authority, evidence sufficiency, escalation, waivers, final approval, and accountability remain assigned. {#tbl-architect-responsibilities tbl-colwidths="[18,26,27,29]"}

The table also clarifies the word *agentic*. The argument does not require every
architecture study to become agentic or autonomous. Agentic
systems[^fn-ml-agentic-system] are useful because they can act on represented
study state, call tools, revise plans, use feedback, and coordinate method
roles. When work iterates, they may participate inside a design loop. Their
ability to act does not assign them organizational responsibility. As these systems become more capable, the authority
delegated to them and the responsible roles around them must be stated more
precisely.

[^fn-ml-agentic-system]: An AI system capable of pursuing complex goals over time, making its own decisions about which tools to use and what steps to take next. Here, such systems participate inside architecture studies with stated authority limits. Permission to act does not make them responsible for organizational decisions.

::: {.callout-architect-checkpoint title="Trace the Decision"}
If an AI-assisted study proposes an invalid artifact, the review record
must show who owned each responsibility. Can you trace which person or
organizational role defined the scope, authorized automated checks, set the
evidence threshold, approved any waiver, and held the commit right for the
authorized transition? If those assignments disappear, the study has hidden the
architecture decision.
:::

## Eliciting and Codifying Tacit Knowledge

If authorized roles must define constraints and accept consequential risk,
those constraints still have to come from somewhere, and much of the time they
are not written down anywhere.

Engineering teams rely on knowledge that does not yet appear in formal
specifications, the tacit knowledge that Polanyi distinguished from knowledge
that can be fully stated [@Polanyi1966TacitDimension]. A packaging engineer may
know that a particular chiplet partition will break the power-delivery mesh. A
compiler lead may know that the current backend in LLVM, a compiler
infrastructure, cannot lower a proposed matrix layout. A senior architect may
anticipate that an accelerator's coherency traffic will starve the XR display
controller. A local proxy may miss all of these integration failures.

Experienced teams actively recover this context before acting. They find the
relevant domain owners, surface undocumented constraints, resolve conflicts,
and decide which parts are valid for the study at hand.

Any delegated participant is limited by the state it can observe and the
interactions it is allowed to use. Missing tacit constraints become
invalid-action and proxy-mismatch risks. An automated participant may stall,
ask for review, or optimize a candidate that violates packaging, compiler,
coherency, or display-timing constraints.

An automated participant can help interview experts, retrieve prior decisions,
and propose candidate constraints, but an authorized domain owner or review
process must validate what enters the study record
(@fig-tacit-extraction-bridge).

Translating hallway conversations and senior-engineer judgment into environment
contracts and rejection conditions allows automation operating within declared limits to reject invalid
designs before they consume expensive simulation or implementation time.

An automated participant cannot treat an elicited statement as authoritative
merely because an expert said it. The review process must resolve conflicts and
record the statement's scope and owner. The record should also state when the
knowledge expires or requires revalidation, including conflicts with another
domain and new contradictory evidence.

![**Reviewing tacit knowledge before using it.** AI can help elicit and organize domain knowledge, but a named domain expert must validate constraints, assumptions, and rejection conditions. Conflicts, expiration, or new evidence require another review.](images/F10-tacit-extraction-bridge){#fig-tacit-extraction-bridge width="100%" fig-alt="Diagram showing domain knowledge passing through AI-assisted elicitation and domain validation before entering represented study state, with conflict, expiry, and new evidence returning the record for revalidation."}

## The Strongest Objections

The proposed approach invites several reasonable objections.

The first objection is that Architecture 2.0 is just good experimental
methodology with new names. Provenance, baselines, rejection criteria, and
held-out validation[^fn-ml-held-out-validation] are not new. That objection is
correct about the lineage. As @sec-moonshot argued, machine learning first
entered computer architecture inside mechanisms and human-specified
methods [@ChenPenneyJimenez2020AIArchitecture], and those uses remain
important. The shift comes when AI also participates in producing the claim
about the design, not only in the design itself. More of the claim-producing
work can now be delegated, so its scope, transformations, evidence limits, and
decision authority must remain traceable.

The approach therefore borrows established forms deliberately. Its structured
evidence record draws from assurance cases and Goal Structuring Notation, a
graphical notation for assurance arguments [@Kelly2004GSN]. Automotive functional safety under ISO 26262 provides a
neighboring standards context [@ISO2018Iso26262]. Its treatment of evidence
sources draws from multi-fidelity modeling[^fn-ml-multi-fidelity]
[@PeherstorferWillcoxGunzburger2018Multifidelity]. Naming these lineages
clarifies what Architecture 2.0 inherits and what computer architecture still
has to build for itself.

[^fn-ml-held-out-validation]: Held-out validation is an established practice. Architecture 2.0 asks that the record state the task split, withheld evidence, and scope of the resulting claim when AI participates in the study.

[^fn-ml-multi-fidelity]: Multi-fidelity modeling is the ancestor of the architect-owned choice among cheap proxies and higher-cost evidence. The consequence and decision rights remain separate from the evidence source.

The second objection is that AI for systems design is overhyped and may add no
value to a particular study. That is sometimes true. The matched run in
@sec-running-the-loop finds no AI-arm advantage and rejects the proposed
mechanism. A framework that requires an AI win would be marketing. A useful
one makes a null result, a simpler baseline, and a stop decision legitimate
outcomes.

The third objection is that the design-loop card adds process overhead that
will not survive a deadline. The card is an optional compact index for studies
where tools or automated participants can advance candidates faster than
reviewers can reconstruct the evidence. A short initial pass can reveal whether
a result rests on a proxy nobody validated or a baseline nobody documented. The
time required and the defects found should be measured in real review settings
rather than assumed.
Skipping the check may move discovery of a weak proxy or undocumented baseline
to a later and more expensive stage. Teams should measure the card's review cost
and the defects it catches rather than assume either value.

The fourth objection is that retaining assigned final decision rights is too
conservative for more capable AI systems. Validated checks can
reject candidates, routine transitions can advance automatically, and
specialized reviews can be delegated. The requirement is that their scope and
authority be declared, exceptions escalate, and consequential commit decisions
remain assigned to a responsible role. This distinction does not depend on a
permanent weakness in current models. Technical competence and organizational
authority are different properties.

These objections limit the scope of the proposal. A design loop is one way to
organize a bounded, iterative study; it is not a goal in itself.

## What the Field Must Maintain

One well-documented study is useful; cumulative knowledge requires maintained
shared infrastructure. The individual resources in
@sec-appendix-c-architecture-20-resources will change, but three requirements
remain:

- **Shared study objects:** Versioned tasks, workload packets, environment
  contracts, matched conventional methods, typed evidence, failures, and
  disclosure boundaries make comparisons interpretable.
- **Credible challenge paths:** Held-out cases, cross-tool checks, qualified
  independent evaluation, conflict rules, waivers, appeals, and retest paths
  give a disputed claim somewhere to go.
- **Lifecycle ownership:** Named maintainers, version policies, archived release
  snapshots, drift checks, revalidation, and retirement rules keep a resource
  from silently changing underneath a claim.

Protected evaluation can satisfy part of this contract without turning private
designs into a public warehouse. It still needs a public account of what a
qualified evaluator inspected, what remained unavailable, and what conclusion
the process supports.

An undifferentiated collection of model output and tool logs is not sufficient.
A reusable record connects a question and baseline to the represented state,
candidate actions, tool observations, failed alternatives, interpretation, and
decision. Logs can provide observations and provenance, but the study must
supply the architecture question, the meaning of a failed check, and the scope
of the claim.

Data without documented tool interfaces are ambiguous. Harnesses without
held-out evaluation can mistake setup differences for method improvements.
Reporting formats without independent review standardize paperwork rather than
evidence. Repositories preserve artifacts, while maintainers define versions,
comparison rules, conflict procedures, appeals, and retest policies. Standards
should follow repeated useful practice rather than freeze today's models.

The stack does not require raw proprietary design disclosure. Its reporting
layer can begin with records that state the task, versions, exposed
interfaces, accepted and rejected candidates, available evidence, redactions,
and named roles. Those records let reviewers ask not only what result was
reported, but which study produced it, what its scope was, and which parts are open to
inspection.

One difficult case is failure knowledge from proprietary physical-design
flows. If those records remain entirely private, outsiders cannot inspect the
evidence and future systems repeatedly encounter the same dead ends. A useful
exchange format would separate protected implementation details from a failure
record with a stated scope, without pretending that the public proxy reproduces
the private result.

One possible approach is a redacted failure record linked to an open or
synthetic proxy. When an industrial AI-assisted study rejects a candidate
because of a proprietary physical-design constraint, the study's execution process
might map the failure to a public task that preserves the rejection lesson
without exposing the design rule. The public record could report the failure
relative to the mapped public task when the mapping preserves the rejection
condition. It must not imply
that the public proxy is equivalent to the proprietary physical-design result.

The context-first pattern in @lst-redacted-trace shows how a study can record
the boundary of an architectural exploration while redacting absolute
dimensions and proprietary design rules. It is a valid schema 2.0 design-loop
card rather than a separate redaction schema. Its context and disclosure
profiles are complete, while inspectability, independent review, and
decision-right profiles are marked partial and replay is not claimed. The
public record names what was attempted and rejected, but another group cannot
audit or replay the protected evidence. It can document scope and omissions,
but it cannot support a public comparison.

Authorized inspection and public replay are separate claims. A protected
reviewer may be able to inspect the evidence even when an outside reader cannot
reproduce the result from public artifacts. The record should name which claim
it supports.

The excerpt below shows the opening fields; the complete validated card ships
with the companion examples and continues with the claim, evidence, failed
check, decision rights, and recorded decision.

```{.yaml #lst-redacted-trace include="../examples/design-loop-cards/redacted-context-v2.yaml" startLine="1" endLine="26" lst-cap="**Redacted Record Excerpt:** The opening fields of a schema-valid v2 card state its public profile gaps, protected context, constraints, and non-claims."}
```

Before using a redacted record for comparison, check that it still states the
task and workload class, action-observation interface, material tool and model
versions, feedback budget, accepted and rejected candidates, evidence status,
redaction boundary, and decision owner. If any comparison-defining field is
hidden, the record can provide context but cannot support a public ranking.

Current benchmarks, datasets, environments, and methods should be judged by the
study properties they support, not by their names or popularity. The versioned
registry in @sec-appendix-c-architecture-20-resources maintains those entries.
Judge each resource by whether it helps a study show its question, AI role,
state, tool path, failures, evidence, disclosure boundary, and named decision
owner.

::: {.callout-war-story title="The public record still could not settle the flagship result"}
**The claim.** AlphaChip is the recurring test case in this material because it is both
influential and disputed
[@MirhoseiniEtAl2021GraphPlacement; @ChengEtAl2023AssessmentRL;
@GoldieEtAl2024ThatChipHasSailed]. The original work reported expert-quality
macro placement and later deployment.

**The gap.** Independent assessments disputed the baselines, proxy, compute comparison,
and reproducibility. Because the strongest results depended on proprietary
designs and training context, no outside group could recreate the full
evaluation.

**The lesson.** Editorial judgment cannot settle the technical dispute. The practical goal is
to make the next one easier to investigate. Shared cases and tools support
public comparison; redacted records state what remains private; and named
reviewers inspect protected evidence. These measures do not replace missing
evidence, but they show where independent verification ends.
:::

The field also needs failure records, including failed runs, rejected candidates, invalid configurations,
stale benchmarks, bad proxies, tool errors, and ideas that were ruled out by
expert judgment. These records are exactly what an AI-assisted study needs if
future systems are to learn from the field's failures rather than rediscover
them. A community that records only successful artifacts gives future systems a
distorted view of architecture practice.

No organization is expected to publish every internal trace. The field can
instead build schemas, examples, synthetic tasks, open benchmarks, redacted
records, and review artifacts that researchers can inspect and compare.
That goal also needs a practical incentive. Teams are more likely to preserve useful failure records
when the record pays for itself by preventing repeated high-fidelity failures,
making handoffs cheaper, supporting review credit, and showing when an old
rejection should be retired rather than repeated forever.

### Start With One Well-Documented Study

A team need not wait for the whole stack. The minimum useful target is one
well-documented study attached to a design review, design-space exploration, or
paper. It should satisfy five study checks:

- **Decision scope:** Name the task and the decision it can inform.
- **Tool path:** Connect at least one tool path to a versioned environment
  contract.
- **Run record:** Preserve successful and failed runs.
- **Evidence boundary:** State what evidence supports the survivor.
- **Decision rights:** Assign automated and human decision rights.

If a second person can reconstruct what was tried, what failed, what advanced
automatically, what required review, and who remains answerable, the record is
already useful. @sec-appendix-a-bootstrapping provides a
step-by-step bootstrap path, with five post-run checks on the execution record.

The study record should also define its lifecycle. A shared failure record needs
reuse limits, revalidation rules, and a retirement path so a local rejection
does not become a false universal rule. Tacit knowledge belongs only where it
can be named as state, rationale, waiver, or owner judgment. Model-side compute
and energy cost, post-silicon telemetry, integrity records, and legal, IP, or
export-control boundaries belong whenever they affect the claim. These are not
extra fields for their own sake; they determine whether the record remains valid
when tools, workloads, models, and owners change.

Education is where the field-building agenda becomes a performance test. Shared
infrastructure matters only if architects can use it to conduct and challenge
better studies.

## What a Graduate Can Do {#sec-what-a-graduate-can-do}

A curriculum for Architecture 2.0 succeeds only if learners can apply what they
learn to new architecture problems. Constructive alignment starts
with intended learning outcomes and aligns teaching activities and assessments
to the performances those outcomes require [@Biggs1996ConstructiveAlignment].
The central learning objective is one integrated test. A reader should
be able to decide where AI assistance is useful, conduct a bounded study, and
defend an evidence-limited recommendation that another architect can challenge.
Each of the six capabilities cashes out as a specific artifact a graduate can
produce, from a study brief for formulation to a defended decision memo for the
final recommendation (@tbl-graduate-capabilities).

| **Capability** | **A graduate can** | **Performance evidence** |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **Formulate** | Bound an unfamiliar architecture problem with a baseline, alternatives, constraints, claim, and non-goals. | Study brief. |
| **Explore** | Choose AI or conventional methods, compare meaningful alternatives, and preserve why serious candidates failed or were rejected. | Alternative set, method rationale, and rejected-candidate record. |
| **Implement** | Produce enough executable structure and tool connectivity to test the claim. | Checkable artifact and tool contract. |
| **Evaluate** | Use representative workloads, matched architecture baselines, justified fidelity, uncertainty, counterevidence, and total feedback cost, plus a conventional or no-AI comparator when benefit from AI is claimed. | Evaluation packet. |
| **Explain** | Form and test a mechanism-level account of why the result occurred. | Mechanism hypothesis plus sensitivity, intervention, or ablation. |
| **Defend** | Make a bounded recommendation with tradeoffs, limits, ownership, and reversal conditions. | Decision memo and oral or written defense. |

: **Observable evidence for the six capabilities.** A learner demonstrates Architecture 2.0 through a bounded architecture study and defensible decision, not through terminology alone. {#tbl-graduate-capabilities tbl-colwidths="[16,48,28]"}

The performance evidence is assessed as a coherent whole using the applicable
review checks below, not by artifact presence or card completion. A justified
finding that AI assistance added no value can satisfy the evaluation outcome;
the learner is not required to manufacture an AI win. The capstone should use a
problem other than the lighthouse case. The learner receives a new,
tractable architecture objective within their domain competence, together with
a feasible tool and evidence budget, and must bound the study. The final record
includes a study brief, alternative and rejection record, method rationale,
checkable artifact and tool path, evaluation packet, tested mechanism
explanation, and a decision memo with stated limits that the learner defends under
independent challenge. @sec-running-the-loop supplies one worked example of an
iterative study turn, and the companion labs provide selected practice. The
open-ended study tests transfer across all six capabilities.

Those outcomes give reviewers a corresponding question. What should they check
in the study records and claims that learners, researchers, and practitioners
produce?

## The Final Practitioner Path

Classic computer architecture relies on quantitative metrics and disciplined
comparison [@HennessyPatterson2017QuantitativeApproach]. The AI-assisted
extension can be summarized in seven steps:

1. **Define the study.** State the architecture question, comparator, claims,
   non-claims, constraints, and decision the evidence may inform.
2. **Identify the limiting resource.** Determine whether representation, legal
   actions, feedback, review capacity, or coordination is slowing the work.
3. **Prepare the inputs and tools.** Version the context, environment, tools, legal
   actions, observations, failures, budgets, and decision rights.
4. **Compare methods fairly.** Match AI and conventional arms on workload,
   tools, resource assumptions, and evaluation opportunity.
5. **Execute and record.** Preserve every material input, output, failed run,
   rejected alternative, amendment, and integrity check.
6. **Evaluate separate claims.** Judge the architecture outcome, AI contribution,
   and mechanism account independently; stop at the evidence actually earned.
7. **Approve the action and define when to revisit it.** Record the recommender, rejector, waivers,
   commit-right holder, residual risk, and observation that would reverse
   the decision.

This path is the six capabilities in operational form. The card indexes it; the
study, evidence, and decision are the substance.

## Research Agenda

Model feature lists will age. Three questions will remain:

1. **When does AI add architectural value?** Matched studies should distinguish
   artifact quality, architectural insight, and decision quality under comparable
   tool, human, compute, and feedback budgets.
2. **What transfers, and when should evidence expire?** Studies should test
   representations, environments, proxies, mechanisms, and failure records
   across workloads, tools, abstraction levels, and technologies, including the
   cases where transfer reverses a ranking or invalidates a claim.
3. **Which challenge and authority arrangements improve decisions?** Controlled
   or field studies should measure error detection, review burden, false accepts
   and rejects, escalation, overreliance, and whether shared or protected
   evaluation can narrow a disputed claim.

Progress is evidence that changes an architectural conclusion, not a larger
model or a longer tool list.

Self-improving design systems provide one demanding case. Future systems may
revise their own representations, propose new tasks, repair tool interfaces,
organize failure records, and recalibrate evidence policies. They may also adapt
toward benchmark gaming, hidden failures, tool overfitting, or biased traces.
Greater capability can change which work is delegated, but it does not make
objectives, evidence standards, exceptions, and consequential authority
self-assigning.

This matters most because the design target no longer holds still. When software
is continuously regenerated and retrained, as @sec-design-loop-no-longer-scales
argued and @sec-loop-patterns-across-stack showed at fleet scale, a specific
artifact ages as the workload it served moves on. The artifact remains what a
team authorizes at a point in time. The lasting value lies in how the study bounds
intent, tests current evidence, rejects unsupported candidates, and reopens a
decision as the target moves.

Earlier Architecture 2.0 work framed the horizon through increasing autonomy
balanced with human expertise [@ReddiYazdanbakhsh2025Architecture20]. This book
makes that balance operational through the scope of delegated authority. Review
which transitions an automated participant may make within stated bounds,
which checks or specialists hold delegated authority, and which consequential
decision rights remain assigned to named people or organizational roles.
That scope, not an autonomy label, is what the architect must keep designing and
maintaining.

## The Architect's Standing Obligation

Across all six capabilities, the architect's standing obligation is to record the
claim, delegated authority, evidence limit, and owner of consequential decisions.
Routine checks need not wait for manual approval, and one person need not hold
every decision right. The study must still show who or what may act, when an
exception escalates, what evidence supports the proposed consequence, and who
remains answerable when the context changes. Replay or reproduction additionally
requires the runnable inputs, environment, provenance, and replay packages appropriate
to the claim.

::: {#pri-human-decision-owner .callout-design-principle title="Name the decision owner"}
Routine checks may be automated or delegated, but consequential decisions
still need a named owner. Record who may advance, reject, escalate, or waive a
result; who has final authority; and who must revisit the decision when the
evidence or context changes.
:::

That principle becomes reviewable through three concrete tests.

::: {.callout-architect-checkpoint title="Standing Obligation"}
- **Assignment test:** Can a reviewer identify the role that set scope, authorized automated checks, judged evidence sufficiency, and held the commit right?
- **Exception test:** Are escalation, waiver, rollback, and reopening paths specified before an exception occurs?
- **Lifecycle test:** When tools, workloads, or owners change, does the record transfer or retire those assignments rather than silently preserving stale authority?
:::

Together, these tests keep decision ownership current as the study, tools, and
organization change.

## Final Thoughts

The opening request for an entire XR subsystem from a short text description
could be read as an attempt to replace the computer architect. The argument
developed here is different. AI expands the work architects can delegate, while
increasing the importance of reviewing architecture claims and assigning
responsibility for decisions.

The tools, models, and automated systems driving this shift will keep changing,
so a catalog of current capabilities will age quickly. Engineering practice
must supply the continuity. In some workflows, generating plausible candidates
will become faster and cheaper than judging them. Those workflows become
rejection-bound when proposal arrival exceeds review capacity. In those cases,
review rather than artifact generation becomes scarce. Not every study is
in that regime, and candidate throughput is not the field's goal. The scarce
result is architectural understanding or judgment that the available evidence
can support.

The lighthouse prompt in @sec-moonshot states an ambitious architecture
request whose apparent simplicity hides workload, interface, tool, evidence,
explanation, and decision requirements. The executed study in
@sec-running-the-loop demonstrates a narrower present standard with one real
model interaction, a matched conventional arm, complete tool results, a null
AI-contribution finding, a falsified mechanism account, network-free replay,
and a decision that remains pending until the responsible person confirms it.

Architecture 2.0 is the engineering discipline of using AI, grounded in
architectural representations, tools, and experiments, to formulate, explore,
implement, evaluate, explain, and defend computer architecture decisions. A
design loop matters when the work iterates because it makes execution and
analysis available for review. Cards, evidence records, checks, and replay
packages can support that work, but they are not goals in themselves. The field advances when architects use AI
to ask better questions, construct and test stronger alternatives, explain why
results occur, and defend decisions whose evidence and limits another architect
can challenge.
